narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your position in Year 3 where you ranked in the third decile among your peers—well below the fifth decile average for those who passed the bar—making it the single strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 1 decile of fourth, compared to the typical sixth decile for successful bar takers, added further weight in the same concerning direction, reinforcing the model's doubts about your trajectory. Even your upper-middle family income bracket, while common among passers (representing about 45% of them), worked against you here by not providing the counterbalancing boost the model expected in light of your grades. Your LSAT score of 36 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20 were close to the averages for bar passers (37.27 and 3.24, respectively), so they didn't sway the decision much either way, nor did your choice to pursue full-time studies, which aligns with most who succeed. These top three factors—your Year 3 and Year 1 class rankings foremost, followed by family background—dominated the prediction, each nudging it toward failure rather than success. In contrast, admitted students typically show higher decile placements early and late in their degrees, often from varied income levels that better complement top grades. While your profile shows promise in some areas, it fell short of the stronger academic deciles that define most accepted applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call based on patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or academic advising to address any Year 1 and 3 hurdles. You might also explore retaking the LSAT for a slight edge or gaining practical experience that bolsters your record. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by focusing on these steps—your next application could tell a different story.
